attributes.stem: A 80-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of severe central chest pain radiating to his left arm. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears distressed with pulse rate 95/min, blood pressure 155/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. His ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads V2-V5. He undergoes emergency coronary angiography with drug-eluting stent insertion to his left anterior descending artery.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate dual antiplatelet therapy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has undergone primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with drug-eluting stent placement for ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is essential to prevent stent thrombosis and future cardiovascular events. Recent Australian cardiac guidelines specifically recommend aspirin plus a potent P2Y12 receptor inhibitor (ticagrelor or prasugrel) as first-line therapy for patients undergoing primary PCI.

Aspirin plus ticagrelor is the preferred DAPT regimen based on evidence from the PLATO trial, which demonstrated superior outcomes compared to aspirin plus clopidogrel in acute coronary syndrome. Ticagrelor is a potent P2Y12 receptor inhibitor with a more rapid onset of action and more predictable antiplatelet effect than clopidogrel. It is now recommended in the latest Australian ACS 2025 guidelines as agent of choice in patients with STEMI who undergo primary PCI.

Aspirin plus clopidogrel was previously the standard DAPT regimen but has been superseded by newer P2Y12 inhibitors (ticagrelor, prasugrel) which have shown superior outcomes in acute coronary syndrome for those undergoing primary PCI. Clopidogrel is a prodrug that requires hepatic metabolism to its active metabolite. It undergoes a two-step oxidation process primarily via cytochrome P450 enzymes, particularly CYP2C19. This metabolic requirement leads to several limitations: slower onset of action (taking 2-6 hours to reach peak effect), variable response due to genetic polymorphisms in CYP2C19 (with up to 30% of patients showing inadequate platelet inhibition), and potential drug interactions with medications that inhibit CYP2C19. These pharmacokinetic limitations, combined with inferior clinical outcomes compared to ticagrelor in reducing cardiovascular death and recurrent ischaemic events, have relegated this combination to second-line therapy or for use in patients undergoing fibrinolysis due to lower bleeding risks.

Aspirin plus prasugrel is also an effective combination but is contraindicated in patients over 75 years, those with prior stroke/TIA, or low body weight (<60 kg) due to increased bleeding risk. Therefore, due to his age, ticagrelor would be preferred as first-line therapy. Note prasugrel is not available in Australia.

Clopidogrel plus ticagrelor or clopidogrel plus prasugrel are inappropriate combinations as they both target the P2Y12 receptor and would increase bleeding risk without additional benefit. Aspirin's different mechanism of action (COX-1 inhibition) makes it the essential backbone of DAPT.

Duration of DAPT depends on the risk of ischaemia balanced against risk of bleeding – generally, patients will be on DAPT between 1-12 months depending on this risk/benefit analysis. The Australian 2025 ACS Guidelines have recently recommended longer term therapy with P2Y12 receptor inhibitor (clopidogrel or ticagrelor) rather than aspirin for single antiplatelet therapy.
learningPoints: Recent Australian cardiac guidelines recommend aspirin plus a potent P2Y12 receptor inhibitor (ticagrelor or prasugrel) as first-line DAPT after primary PCI for STEMI.
Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients over 75 years or with prior stroke/TIA due to increased bleeding risk.
Clopidogrel has slower onset, more variable response, and inferior outcomes compared to newer P2Y12 inhibitors in acute coronary syndrome.
references: 2017 ESC focused update on dual antiplatelet therapy in coronary artery disease developed in collaboration with EACTS
National Heart Foundation of Australia & Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand. "ACS Guideline 2025." Heart Foundation, 2025, www.heartfoundation.org.au/for-professionals/acs-guideline?tab=0.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
